        # Combine spatial and temporal components
        for i in 1:nx, j in 1:ny
            intensity_field[i, j, t] = exp(μ + σ_s * spatial_field[i, j] + temporal_field[t])
        end
    end
    
    return intensity_field, x_grid, y_grid, t_grid
end

# Simulate disease outbreak intensity
μ = -2.0      # baseline log-intensity
σ_s = 1.0     # spatial variance
α_s = 2.0     # spatial range
σ_t = 0.5     # temporal variance  
α_t = 10.0    # temporal range
T = 50.0      # time horizon

intensity, x_grid, y_grid, t_grid = simulate_spacetime_lgcp(μ, σ_s, α_s, σ_t, α_t, T, 50, 50, 50)

# Visualize intensity at three time points
times_to_plot = [10, 25, 40]
p = []
for (i, t_idx) in enumerate([10, 25, 40])
    push!(p, heatmap(x_grid, y_grid, intensity[:, :, t_idx]', 
                    title="t = $(t_grid[t_idx])", colorbar_title="λ(s,t)"))
end
plot(p..., layout=(1, 3), size=(900, 300))

5.4 7.4 Hawkes Processes and Self-Excitation

Self-exciting point processes capture phenomena where events increase the probability of subsequent events nearby in space and time. The Hawkes process provides the canonical framework for such dependencies.

Definition 7.6 (Spatio-Temporal Hawkes Process). A spatio-temporal point process N with history ℋ_t has conditional intensity

λ(s, t | ℋ_t) = μ(s, t) + ∑_{t_i < t} g(s - s_i, t - t_i)

where μ(s, t) ≥ 0 is the background intensity and g(s, τ) ≥ 0 is the triggering kernel representing the excitation effect of past events.

The triggering kernel typically decays with distance and time. A common parametrization is:

g(s, τ) = α · f_s(s) · f_t(τ)

where α > 0 controls the excitation strength, f_s(s) is a spatial kernel (often Gaussian), and f_t(τ) is a temporal kernel (often exponential decay).

Theorem 7.2 (Stability Condition). A spatio-temporal Hawkes process is stable (has finite expected intensity) if and only if

∫₀^∞ ∫_{ℝᵈ} g(s, τ) ds dτ < 1

Proof. The condition ensures that each event triggers, on average, fewer than one additional event, preventing explosive growth. The proof follows by analyzing the branching structure of triggered events (Daley and Vere-Jones, 2003). □

using StatsBase

"""
Simulate spatio-temporal Hawkes process using thinning algorithm
"""
function simulate_hawkes_spacetime(μ₀, α, β, σ, T, region_bounds)
    events = Tuple{Float64, Float64, Float64}[]  # (x, y, t)
    xmin, xmax, ymin, ymax = region_bounds
    area = (xmax - xmin) * (ymax - ymin)
    
    # Upper bound for intensity (conservative estimate)
    λ_max = μ₀ * area + α * 100  # heuristic upper bound
    
    t = 0.0
    while t < T
        # Generate candidate event time
        dt = -log(rand()) / λ_max
        t += dt
        
        if t >= T
            break
        end
        
        # Generate candidate location
        x = rand(Uniform(xmin, xmax))
        y = rand(Uniform(ymin, ymax))
        
        # Calculate actual intensity at (x, y, t)
        background = μ₀
        excitement = 0.0
        
        for (x_prev, y_prev, t_prev) in events
            if t_prev < t
                # Spatial kernel: Gaussian
                spatial_dist = sqrt((x - x_prev)^2 + (y - y_prev)^2)
                spatial_kernel = exp(-spatial_dist^2 / (2 * σ^2))
                
                # Temporal kernel: exponential decay
                temporal_kernel = exp(-β * (t - t_prev))
                
                excitement += α * spatial_kernel * temporal_kernel
            end
        end
        
        λ_current = background + excitement
        
        # Accept/reject step
        if rand() < λ_current / λ_max
            push!(events, (x, y, t))
        end
    end
    
    return events
end

# Simulate crime incidents with self-excitation
Random.seed!(456)
μ₀ = 0.1      # background rate per unit area per unit time
α = 2.0       # excitation strength
β = 0.2       # temporal decay rate  
σ = 1.0       # spatial excitation range
T = 100.0     # time horizon
region = (0.0, 20.0, 0.0, 20.0)

events = simulate_hawkes_spacetime(μ₀, α, β, σ, T, region)
println("Generated $(length(events)) events")

# Extract coordinates for plotting
x_coords = [e[1] for e in events]
y_coords = [e[2] for e in events]
times = [e[3] for e in events]

# Plot events colored by time
scatter(x_coords, y_coords, c=times, 
        xlabel="X", ylabel="Y", colorbar_title="Time",
        title="Hawkes Process: Crime Incidents", markersize=3)

5.5 7.5 ETAS Model for Earthquake Sequences

The Epidemic-Type Aftershock Sequence (ETAS) model represents a specialized Hawkes process designed for earthquake modeling. It incorporates magnitude-dependent triggering and power-law decay kernels that match observed seismic patterns.

Definition 7.7 (ETAS Model). The ETAS model has conditional intensity

λ(s, t | ℋ_t) = μ(s) + ∑_{t_i < t, M_i ≥ M₀} K(M_i) · f(s - s_i) · h(t - t_i)

where: - μ(s) is the background seismicity rate - K(M_i) = A exp(α(M_i - M₀)) is magnitude-dependent productivity - f(s) = γ(s² + d²)^(-q) is the spatial triggering kernel
- h(τ) = p(τ + c)^(-p) is the temporal triggering kernel (Omori-Utsu law)

The parameters have seismological interpretations: - A: overall aftershock productivity - α: magnitude scaling (typically α ≈ ln(10) ≈ 2.3) - γ, d, q: spatial clustering parameters - p, c: temporal clustering parameters (typically p > 1)

Theorem 7.3 (ETAS Stability). The ETAS model is stable when A ∫_{M₀}^∞ exp(α(m - M₀)) dG(m) < 1, where G(m) is the magnitude distribution.

Proof. This follows from Theorem 7.2 by integrating the triggering kernel over space, time, and magnitude. For the Gutenberg-Richter law dG(m) = b ln(10) exp(-b ln(10)(m - M₀)) dm with b > α/ln(10), the integral converges to a finite value. □

"""
Simulate ETAS earthquake sequence
"""
function simulate_etas(μ, A, α, γ, d, q, p, c, b, M₀, T, region_bounds)
    events = []  # Will store (x, y, t, magnitude)
    xmin, xmax, ymin, ymax = region_bounds
    
    # Background rate (assume uniform)
    area = (xmax - xmin) * (ymax - ymax)
    background_rate = μ * area
    
    t = 0.0
    while t < T
        # Calculate current total intensity (upper bound)
        current_background = background_rate
        current_triggered = 0.0
        
        # Approximate triggered rate (for efficiency in simulation)
        for event in events
            x_prev, y_prev, t_prev, M_prev = event
            if t_prev < t && M_prev >= M₀
                productivity = A * exp(α * (M_prev - M₀))
                temporal = p * (t - t_prev + c)^(-p)
                # Integrate spatial kernel over region (approximation)
                spatial_integral = γ * π * d^(2-q) / (q-2) # for q > 2
                current_triggered += productivity * temporal * spatial_integral
            end
        end
        
        total_rate = current_background + current_triggered
        
        # Generate next event time
        if total_rate <= 0
            break
        end
        
        dt = -log(rand()) / total_rate
        t += dt
        
        if t >= T
            break
        end
        
        # Determine if background or triggered event
        if rand() < current_background / total_rate
            # Background event
            x = rand(Uniform(xmin, xmax))
            y = rand(Uniform(ymin, ymax))
        else
            # Triggered event - select triggering event
            weights = Float64[]
            for event in events
                x_prev, y_prev, t_prev, M_prev = event
                if t_prev < t && M_prev >= M₀
                    productivity = A * exp(α * (M_prev - M₀))
                    temporal = p * (t - t_prev + c)^(-p)
                    push!(weights, productivity * temporal)
                else
                    push!(weights, 0.0)
                end
            end
            
            if sum(weights) > 0
                # Sample triggering event
                triggering_idx = sample(1:length(events), Weights(weights))
                x_trig, y_trig, _, _ = events[triggering_idx]
                
                # Sample location around triggering event
                # Use rejection sampling for spatial kernel
                while true
                    r = sqrt(rand()) * 5 * d  # sample radius
                    θ = 2π * rand()           # sample angle
                    x = x_trig + r * cos(θ)
                    y = y_trig + r * sin(θ)
                    
                    if xmin <= x <= xmax && ymin <= y <= ymax
                        spatial_prob = γ * (r^2 + d^2)^(-q)
                        if rand() < spatial_prob / (γ * d^(-2q))  # normalized
                            break
                        end
                    end
                end
            else
                # Fallback to background
                x = rand(Uniform(xmin, xmax))
                y = rand(Uniform(ymin, ymax))
            end
        end
        
        # Generate magnitude from Gutenberg-Richter distribution
        magnitude = M₀ - log(rand()) / (b * log(10))
        
        push!(events, (x, y, t, magnitude))
    end
    
    return events
end

# Simulate California-like earthquake sequence
Random.seed!(789)
μ = 0.01        # background rate
A = 0.05        # productivity parameter
α = 2.3         # magnitude scaling
γ = 0.1         # spatial kernel amplitude
d = 1.0         # spatial kernel range
q = 1.5         # spatial kernel exponent
p = 1.2         # temporal kernel exponent
c = 0.01        # temporal kernel shift
b = 1.0         # Gutenberg-Richter b-value
M₀ = 3.0        # minimum magnitude
T = 365.0       # one year
region = (0.0, 100.0, 0.0, 100.0)  # 100km × 100km

earthquakes = simulate_etas(μ, A, α, γ, d, q, p, c, b, M₀, T, region)
println("Simulated $(length(earthquakes)) earthquakes")

# Extract data for analysis
x_eq = [eq[1] for eq in earthquakes]
y_eq = [eq[2] for eq in earthquakes]
t_eq = [eq[3] for eq in earthquakes]
m_eq = [eq[4] for eq in earthquakes]

# Plot earthquakes with symbol size proportional to magnitude
scatter(x_eq, y_eq, markersize=2*exp.(0.5*(m_eq .- M₀)), 
        c=t_eq, alpha=0.7,
        xlabel="X (km)", ylabel="Y (km)", 
        colorbar_title="Time (days)",
        title="ETAS Earthquake Sequence")

5.6 7.6 Conditional Intensity Estimation

Estimating conditional intensity functions from spatio-temporal data requires careful handling of the history dependence. Maximum likelihood estimation provides the standard approach, but computational challenges arise from the integral terms in the log-likelihood.

For a spatio-temporal point process observed on region W × [0, T] with events {(s_i, t_i), i = 1, …, n}, the log-likelihood is:

ℓ(θ) = ∑ᵢ₌₁ⁿ log λ(s_i, t_i | ℋ_{t_i}; θ) - ∫₀ᵀ ∫_W λ(s, t | ℋ_t; θ) ds dt

The integral term represents the compensator and requires numerical approximation in most cases.

Theorem 7.4 (Consistency of MLE). Under regularity conditions, the maximum likelihood estimator θ̂ for the conditional intensity parameters is consistent and asymptotically normal as the observation region W expands.

using Optim, ForwardDiff

"""
Fit simple Hawkes process parameters via maximum likelihood
"""
function fit_hawkes_mle(events, T, region_bounds)
    xmin, xmax, ymin, ymax = region_bounds
    area = (xmax - xmin) * (ymax - ymin)
    
    # Extract event times and locations
    times = [e[3] for e in events]
    locations = [(e[1], e[2]) for e in events]
    n_events = length(events)
    
    # Negative log-likelihood function
    function neg_loglik(params)
        μ₀, α, β, σ = params
        
        if μ₀ <= 0 || α < 0 || β <= 0 || σ <= 0
            return Inf
        end
        
        ll = 0.0
        
        # Sum over observed events
        for i in 1:n_events
            ti = times[i]
            si = locations[i]
            
            # Background intensity
            λ_i = μ₀
            
            # Triggered intensity from previous events
            for j in 1:(i-1)
                tj = times[j]
                sj = locations[j]
                
                if tj < ti
                    spatial_dist = sqrt((si[1] - sj[1])^2 + (si[2] - sj[2])^2)
                    kernel = α * exp(-spatial_dist^2 / (2*σ^2)) * exp(-β * (ti - tj))
                    λ_i += kernel
                end
            end
            
            ll += log(λ_i)
        end
        
        # Subtract compensator (numerical approximation)
        # Use simple time discretization
        dt = T / 1000
        compensator = 0.0
        
        for k in 1:1000
            t = k * dt
            
            # Background contribution
            compensator += μ₀ * area * dt
            
            # Triggered contribution
            for i in 1:n_events
                if times[i] < t
                    ti = times[i]
                    excitation_integral = α * (2π * σ^2) * (1 - exp(-β * (t - ti))) / β
                    compensator += excitation_integral * dt
                end
            end
        end
        
        return -(ll - compensator)
    end
    
    # Initial parameter guess
    initial_params = [n_events/(area * T), 0.5, 0.1, 1.0]
    
    # Optimize
    result = optimize(neg_loglik, initial_params, BFGS())
    
    return result.minimizer, result.minimum
end

# Fit ETAS model to simulated data
if length(earthquakes) > 10  # Ensure sufficient data
    params_est, neg_ll = fit_hawkes_mle(earthquakes, T, region)
    μ_est, α_est, β_est, σ_est = params_est
    
    println("Estimated parameters:")
    println("μ₀ = $(round(μ_est, digits=4)) (true: $μ₀)")
    println("α = $(round(α_est, digits=4)) (true: $α)")  
    println("β = $(round(β_est, digits=4)) (true: $β)")
    println("σ = $(round(σ_est, digits=4)) (true: $σ)")
end

5.7 7.7 Residual Analysis and Model Validation

Residual analysis for spatio-temporal point processes extends the transformed time approach from Chapter 5. The key insight is to transform each event time using the conditional intensity to create a homogeneous Poisson process under the correct model.

Definition 7.8 (Spatio-Temporal Residuals). For a fitted conditional intensity λ̂(s, t | ℋ_t), the residual process has events at times

τ_i = ∫₀^{t_i} ∫_W λ̂(s, u | ℋ_u) ds du

If the model is correct, the τ_i should follow a homogeneous Poisson process on [0, Λ(T)] where Λ(T) is the total compensator.

This transformation allows standard Poisson process diagnostics: exponential inter-arrival times, uniform spacings, and Q-Q plots against theoretical distributions.

"""
Compute spatio-temporal residuals for model validation
"""
function compute_spacetime_residuals(events, T, region_bounds, λ_func)
    times = sort([e[3] for e in events])
    n_events = length(times)
    residuals = zeros(n_events)
    
    xmin, xmax, ymin, ymax = region_bounds
    area = (xmax - xmin) * (ymax - ymin)
    
    # Compute cumulative intensity for each event
    for i in 1:n_events
        # Integrate intensity from 0 to t_i
        dt = times[i] / 1000  # discretization step
        cumulative = 0.0
        
        for k in 1:1000
            t = k * dt
            if t <= times[i]
                # Integrate over space at time t
                # Use fitted intensity function
                λ_total = λ_func(t, events[1:findfirst(e -> e[3] >= t, events)])
                cumulative += λ_total * area * dt
            end
        end
        
        residuals[i] = cumulative
    end
    
    return residuals
end

"""
Simple intensity function for residual computation
"""
function fitted_intensity(t, history_events)
    # Use estimated parameters from previous fit
    if @isdefined μ_est
        background = μ_est
        excitement = 0.0
        
        for event in history_events
            if event[3] < t
                excitement += α_est * exp(-β_est * (t - event[3]))
            end
        end
        
        return background + excitement
    else
        return 0.1  # fallback
    end
end

# Compute residuals if we have parameter estimates
if @isdefined μ_est && length(earthquakes) > 5
    residuals = compute_spacetime_residuals(earthquakes, T, region, fitted_intensity)
    
    # Test for exponential inter-arrival times
    inter_arrivals = diff(residuals)
    
    # Q-Q plot against exponential distribution
    theoretical_quantiles = quantile(Exponential(1), (1:length(inter_arrivals)) / (length(inter_arrivals) + 1))
    empirical_quantiles = sort(inter_arrivals)
    
    scatter(theoretical_quantiles, empirical_quantiles,
            xlabel="Theoretical Quantiles", ylabel="Empirical Quantiles",
            title="Q-Q Plot: Residual Inter-arrivals vs Exponential(1)",
            aspect_ratio=:equal)
    plot!([0, maximum(theoretical_quantiles)], [0, maximum(theoretical_quantiles)], 
          color=:red, linestyle=:dash)
end

5.8 7.8 Applications in Crime Analysis and Social Media

Crime analysis represents a primary application domain for spatio-temporal point processes. Criminal events exhibit strong clustering patterns: certain locations (“hotspots”) have elevated risk, and recent crimes increase the probability of subsequent incidents nearby through various mechanisms including repeat victimization and offender mobility patterns.

The self-exciting Hawkes process captures these dynamics naturally. Consider burglary data where the conditional intensity takes the form:

λ(s, t | ℋ_t) = μ(s, t) + ∑_{i: t_i < t} α_i f(s - s_i) g(t - t_i)

where μ(s, t) represents long-term risk patterns (possibly incorporating socioeconomic covariates), f(·) captures spatial influence (typically Gaussian with range 100-500 meters), and g(·) represents temporal decay (typically exponential with half-life 1-2 weeks).

Social media event propagation provides another rich application area. Consider how news stories, viral content, or social movements spread through geographic networks. The spatio-temporal intensity might incorporate both distance-based diffusion and network connectivity:

λ(s, t | ℋ_t) = μ(s, t) + ∑_{i: t_i < t} α(s_i, s) β(m_i) exp(-δ(t - t_i))

where α(s_i, s) captures spatial influence (possibly incorporating transportation networks or demographic similarity), β(m_i) represents content attractiveness, and δ controls temporal decay rates.

5.9 7.9 Connections and Extensions

The spatio-temporal point process framework developed in this chapter connects to numerous other areas of the curriculum. The conditional intensity functions relate \newpage

6 Chapter 8: Point Process Regression and Modern Methods

The statistical analysis of spatial point patterns often requires understanding how point intensities vary across space in response to environmental covariates. In previous chapters, we developed the theory of point processes as mathematical objects. We now turn to the inferential problem: how can we estimate intensity functions from observed point patterns and relate them to spatial covariates? This chapter develops modern statistical methods for point process regression, connecting classical spatial statistics with contemporary machine learning approaches.

The fundamental insight is that point process intensity can be modeled as a function of spatial covariates, much like regression models relate response variables to predictors. However, the discrete nature of point patterns and the continuous nature of intensity functions create unique challenges that require specialized methods.

6.1 Intensity as a Function of Spatial Covariates

Consider a spatial point process with intensity function λ(s) at location s ∈ W ⊂ ℝ². In regression contexts, we model this intensity as depending on p-dimensional covariate vectors z(s) = (z₁(s), z₂(s), …, zₚ(s))ᵀ observed at each location s. The fundamental regression relationship takes the form:

λ(s; β) = g(z(s)ᵀβ)

where β = (β₁, β₂, …, βₚ)ᵀ are regression coefficients and g(·) is a link function ensuring λ(s; β) > 0.

Definition 8.1 (Point Process Regression Model). A point process regression model specifies the intensity function λ(s; β) = g(z(s)ᵀβ) where z(s) is a p-dimensional covariate vector at location s, β is a parameter vector, and g: ℝ → ℝ⁺ is a link function.

The most common choice is the log-link g(u) = exp(u), yielding:

λ(s; β) = exp(z(s)ᵀβ) = exp(β₁z₁(s) + β₂z₂(s) + ⋯ + βₚzₚ(s))

This ensures positivity of the intensity and provides multiplicative effects of covariates.

6.1.1 Maximum Likelihood Estimation via Poisson Regression

For a Poisson point process with intensity λ(s; β) observed in window W, the likelihood is:

L(β) = exp(-∫_W λ(s; β) ds) ∏ᵢ₌₁ⁿ λ(xᵢ; β)

where x₁, …, xₙ are the observed points. Taking logarithms:

ℓ(β) = Σᵢ₌₁ⁿ log λ(xᵢ; β) - ∫_W λ(s; β) ds

With the log-link, this becomes:

ℓ(β) = Σᵢ₌₁ⁿ z(xᵢ)ᵀβ - ∫_W exp(z(s)ᵀβ) ds

The integral presents computational challenges, but can be approximated using quadrature methods.

Theorem 8.1 (Quadrature Approximation for Point Process Regression). Let {s₁, s₂, …, sₘ} be a fine grid of points in W with associated weights {w₁, w₂, …, wₘ} such that ∫_W f(s) ds ≈ Σⱼ₌₁ᵐ wⱼf(sⱼ) for smooth functions f. Then the log-likelihood can be approximated as:

ℓ(β) ≈ Σᵢ₌₁ⁿ z(xᵢ)ᵀβ - Σⱼ₌₁ᵐ wⱼ exp(z(sⱼ)ᵀβ)

Proof. The approximation follows directly from numerical integration theory. The key insight is that this transforms the problem into a standard Poisson regression by treating each observed point as having “response” 1 and each quadrature point as having “response” 0, with offset terms log(wⱼ) for quadrature points. □

This equivalence allows us to use standard generalized linear model software, treating the problem as Poisson regression with n + m observations: n points with response 1 and weights 1, and m quadrature points with response 0 and weights wⱼ.

6.1.2 Computational Implementation

using GLM, DataFrames, Distributions, Random

"""
Fit point process regression using quadrature approximation.
Returns fitted GLM object.
"""
function fit_point_process_regression(points, covariates_at_points, 
                                    quadrature_points, quadrature_weights, 
                                    covariates_at_quad)
    n_points = size(points, 1)
    n_quad = size(quadrature_points, 1)
    
    # Combine responses: 1 for observed points, 0 for quadrature
    y = vcat(ones(Int, n_points), zeros(Int, n_quad))
    
    # Combine covariates
    X = vcat(covariates_at_points, covariates_at_quad)
    
    # Combine weights: 1 for points, quadrature weights for quad points
    weights = vcat(ones(n_points), quadrature_weights)
    
    # Create dataframe
    df = DataFrame(X, :auto)
    df.y = y
    df.weights = weights
    
    # Fit Poisson GLM with log link
    formula_terms = Symbol.(names(df)[1:end-2])  # Exclude y and weights
    formula = Term(:y) ~ sum(Term.(formula_terms))
    
    glm(formula, df, Poisson(), LogLink(), wts=df.weights)
end

# Example: Simulate point pattern with environmental gradient
Random.seed!(123)
n = 100
window = (0.0, 10.0, 0.0, 10.0)  # (xmin, xmax, ymin, ymax)

# Generate points with intensity depending on x-coordinate
# λ(x,y) = exp(0.5 + 0.2*x)
function simulate_inhomogeneous_poisson(window, intensity_fun, max_points=1000)
    xmin, xmax, ymin, ymax = window
    area = (xmax - xmin) * (ymax - ymin)
    
    # Upper bound for intensity
    λ_max = maximum([intensity_fun(x, y) 
                    for x in xmin:0.1:xmax, y in ymin:0.1:ymax])
    
    # Generate candidate points from homogeneous Poisson
    n_candidates = rand(Poisson(λ_max * area))
    candidates_x = rand(Uniform(xmin, xmax), n_candidates)
    candidates_y = rand(Uniform(ymin, ymax), n_candidates)
    
    # Thin using acceptance probability
    accepted_points = []
    for i in 1:n_candidates
        if rand() < intensity_fun(candidates_x[i], candidates_y[i]) / λ_max
            push!(accepted_points, (candidates_x[i], candidates_y[i]))
        end
    end
    
    return hcat([[p[1] for p in accepted_points] [p[2] for p in accepted_points]]...)
end

intensity_fun(x, y) = exp(0.5 + 0.2*x)
points = simulate_inhomogeneous_poisson(window, intensity_fun)

# Create covariate matrix for observed points
X_points = hcat(ones(size(points, 1)), points[:, 1])  # Intercept + x-coordinate

# Create quadrature grid
quad_x = repeat(0.5:0.5:9.5, inner=20)
quad_y = repeat(0.5:0.5:9.5, outer=20)
quad_points = hcat(quad_x, quad_y)
quad_weights = fill(0.25, length(quad_x))  # Each quadrature point represents 0.5×0.5 area

# Covariates at quadrature points
X_quad = hcat(ones(size(quad_points, 1)), quad_points[:, 1])

# Fit model
model = fit_point_process_regression(points, X_points, quad_points, 
                                   quad_weights, X_quad)

println("Fitted coefficients:")
println("Intercept: $(coef(model)[1]) (true: 0.5)")
println("x-slope: $(coef(model)[2]) (true: 0.2)")

6.2 Logistic Regression for Presence/Absence Data

When point process data is aggregated into spatial units (pixels, grid cells, or regions), the analysis often focuses on presence/absence rather than counts. Let Yᵢ ∈ {0, 1} indicate whether region i contains at least one event, and let zᵢ be the covariate vector for region i.

The probability of presence in region i with area Aᵢ under a Poisson point process with intensity λᵢ = exp(zᵢᵀβ) is:

P(Yᵢ = 1) = 1 - P(Yᵢ = 0) = 1 - exp(-λᵢAᵢ) = 1 - exp(-Aᵢ exp(zᵢᵀβ))

This is not a standard logistic regression model. However, when λᵢAᵢ is small (rare events in small regions), we can use the approximation:

P(Yᵢ = 1) ≈ λᵢAᵢ = Aᵢ exp(zᵢᵀβ)

With the logit link, this leads to:

logit(P(Yᵢ = 1)) = log(Aᵢ) + zᵢᵀβ

where log(Aᵢ) serves as an offset term.

Theorem 8.2 (Complementary Log-Log Link for Point Process Presence/Absence). For presence/absence data derived from a Poisson point process, the exact relationship is: P(Yᵢ = 1) = 1 - exp(-Aᵢ exp(zᵢᵀβ))

This corresponds to a generalized linear model with complementary log-log link: cloglog(P(Yᵢ = 1)) = log(Aᵢ) + zᵢᵀβ where cloglog(p) = log(-log(1-p)).

Proof. Starting from P(Yᵢ = 1) = 1 - exp(-Aᵢ exp(zᵢᵀβ)), we have: 1 - P(Yᵢ = 1) = exp(-Aᵢ exp(zᵢᵀβ)) Taking logarithms: log(1 - P(Yᵢ = 1)) = -Aᵢ exp(zᵢᵀβ) Taking logarithms again: log(-log(1 - P(Yᵢ = 1))) = log(Aᵢ) + zᵢᵀβ □

6.2.1 Computational Example

using GLM, DataFrames, StatsBase

"""
Generate presence/absence data from underlying point process
"""
function generate_presence_absence(grid_size, cell_area, intensity_fun)
    presence = zeros(Int, grid_size, grid_size)
    
    for i in 1:grid_size, j in 1:grid_size
        # Cell center coordinates
        x = (i - 0.5) * sqrt(cell_area)
        y = (j - 0.5) * sqrt(cell_area)
        
        # Expected number of points in this cell
        λ = intensity_fun(x, y) * cell_area
        
        # Generate presence/absence
        presence[i, j] = rand(Poisson(λ)) > 0 ? 1 : 0
    end
    
    return presence
end

# Generate data
Random.seed!(42)
grid_size = 20
cell_area = 0.25
intensity_fun(x, y) = exp(-0.5 + 0.3*x - 0.1*y)

presence_matrix = generate_presence_absence(grid_size, cell_area, intensity_fun)

# Create dataframe for regression
n_cells = grid_size^2
df = DataFrame(
    x = repeat(1:grid_size, outer=grid_size) .- 0.5,
    y = repeat(1:grid_size, inner=grid_size) .- 0.5,
    presence = vec(presence_matrix'),
    area = fill(cell_area, n_cells)
)

# Fit logistic regression with logit link (approximate)
logistic_model = glm(@formula(presence ~ x + y), df, Binomial(), LogitLink())

# Fit with complementary log-log link (exact for Poisson process)
cloglog_model = glm(@formula(presence ~ x + y + offset(log(area))), 
                   df, Binomial(), CloglogLink())

println("Logistic regression coefficients:")
println(coef(logistic_model))
println("\nComplementary log-log coefficients:")
println(coef(cloglog_model))
println("True coefficients: [-0.5, 0.3, -0.1]")

6.3 Regularized Intensity Estimation

When dealing with high-dimensional covariate spaces or when seeking sparse models, regularization becomes essential. The LASSO (Least Absolute Shrinkage and Selection Operator) penalty can be applied to point process regression by adding an L₁ penalty to the log-likelihood.

The penalized log-likelihood for point process regression becomes:

ℓₚₑₙ(β) = ℓ(β) - α Σⱼ₌₁ᵖ |βⱼ|

where α ≥ 0 is the regularization parameter and the penalty is applied to all coefficients except the intercept.

Definition 8.2 (LASSO Point Process Regression). The LASSO estimator for point process regression is: β̂ₗₐₛₛₒ(α) = argmax_β {ℓ(β) - α Σⱼ₌₂ᵖ |βⱼ|} where ℓ(β) is the point process log-likelihood and α ≥ 0 controls the strength of regularization.

The LASSO penalty encourages sparsity by shrinking small coefficients to exactly zero, effectively performing variable selection. This is particularly valuable when many spatial covariates are available but only a subset are truly influential.

6.3.1 Cross-Validation for Regularization Parameter Selection

The regularization parameter α is typically chosen via cross-validation. For spatial point processes, care must be taken to account for spatial correlation when creating cross-validation folds.

using GLMNet, Random, LinearAlgebra

"""
Fit LASSO point process regression using coordinate descent
"""
function fit_lasso_point_process(points, covariates_at_points, 
                               quadrature_points, quadrature_weights, 
                               covariates_at_quad; α_values = 10.0.^(-3:0.1:1))
    
    n_points = size(points, 1)
    n_quad = size(quadrature_points, 1)
    
    # Create combined dataset (similar to earlier function)
    y = vcat(ones(n_points), zeros(n_quad))
    X = vcat(covariates_at_points, covariates_at_quad)
    weights = vcat(ones(n_points), quadrature_weights)
    
    # Fit LASSO path using GLMNet (treating as weighted Poisson regression)
    fit = glmnet(X[:, 2:end], y, Poisson(), 
                weights = weights,
                alpha = 1.0,  # Pure LASSO
                lambda = α_values,
                offset = X[:, 1])  # Intercept as offset
    
    return fit
end

# Example with simulated high-dimensional data
Random.seed!(789)
n = 200
p = 50  # High-dimensional covariate space

# True sparse coefficient vector (only first 5 covariates matter)
β_true = vcat([2.0, -1.5, 1.0, -0.8, 0.6], zeros(p-5))

# Generate spatial covariates (spatially smooth using RBF kernel)
function generate_spatial_covariates(n_locations, n_covariates)
    locations = rand(n_locations, 2) .* 10  # Points in [0,10]²
    
    # Create spatially smooth covariates using random Fourier features
    covariates = zeros(n_locations, n_covariates)
    
    for j in 1:n_covariates
        # Random frequencies and phases for spatial smoothness
        n_freqs = 5
        freqs = randn(n_freqs, 2) .* 0.5
        phases = rand(n_freqs) .* 2π
        
        for i in 1:n_locations
            covariates[i, j] = sum(cos.(sum(freqs .* locations[i:i, :]', dims=2) .+ phases)) / sqrt(n_freqs)
        end
    end
    
    return locations, covariates
end

# Generate point pattern with sparse intensity function
locations_all = rand(10000, 2) .* 10
_, covariates_all = generate_spatial_covariates(10000, p)
X_all = hcat(ones(10000), covariates_all)

# Compute intensity at all locations
λ_all = exp.(X_all * β_true)
λ_max = maximum(λ_all)

# Thin to create actual point pattern
n_candidates = 10000
accepted_indices = []
for i in 1:n_candidates
    if rand() < λ_all[i] / λ_max
        push!(accepted_indices, i)
    end
end

points = locations_all[accepted_indices, :]
X_points = X_all[accepted_indices, :]

# Create quadrature approximation
quad_indices = sample(1:10000, 2000, replace=false)  # Random subsample for quadrature
quad_points = locations_all[quad_indices, :]
X_quad = X_all[quad_indices, :]
quad_weights = fill(100.0 / 2000, 2000)  # Total area 100, divided by 2000 points

# Fit LASSO model
println("Fitting LASSO point process regression...")
lasso_fit = fit_lasso_point_process(points, X_points, quad_points, 
                                  quad_weights, X_quad)

# Extract coefficients at different regularization levels
for (i, λ) in enumerate(lasso_fit.lambda[1:5:end])
    coefs = lasso_fit.betas[:, i]
    n_nonzero = sum(abs.(coefs) .> 1e-6)
    println("λ = $λ: $n_nonzero nonzero coefficients")
end

6.4 Machine Learning for Intensity Estimation

Traditional parametric models for point process intensity assume specific functional forms (typically log-linear). Modern machine learning methods can capture complex nonlinear relationships between covariates and intensity without strong distributional assumptions.

6.4.1 Random Forests for Point Process Intensity

Random forests can be adapted for point process regression by treating the problem as weighted regression, where observed point locations receive weight 1 and quadrature points receive negative weights proportional to their quadrature weights.

using MLJ, DecisionTree, Random

"""
Fit random forest for point process intensity using quadrature
"""
function fit_rf_point_process(points, covariates_at_points,
                            quadrature_points, quadrature_weights,
                            covariates_at_quad; n_trees=100)
    
    # Combine datasets
    X_combined = vcat(covariates_at_points, covariates_at_quad)
    
    # Create "pseudo-responses" for random forest
    # Points get positive weights, quadrature gets negative
    n_points = size(points, 1)
    n_quad = size(quadrature_points, 1)
    
    # Use log-link: pseudo-response is log-intensity
    y_points = zeros(n_points)  # Will be updated iteratively
    y_quad = zeros(n_quad)
    
    # Initial intensity estimate (constant)
    λ_init = n_points / sum(quadrature_weights)
    y_combined = vcat(fill(log(λ_init), n_points), fill(log(λ_init), n_quad))
    weights = vcat(ones(n_points), quadrature_weights)
    
    # Fit random forest using iterative procedure (simplified Newton-Raphson)
    RandomForestRegressor = @load RandomForestRegressor pkg=DecisionTree
    rf_model = RandomForestRegressor(n_trees=n_trees)
    
    for iter in 1:5  # Few iterations usually sufficient
        # Fit current model
        mach = machine(rf_model, DataFrame(X_combined, :auto), y_combined, weights)
        fit!(mach)
        
        # Predict log-intensity
        log_λ_pred = predict(mach, DataFrame(X_combined, :auto))
        λ_pred = exp.(log_λ_pred)
        
        # Update pseudo-responses (Newton-Raphson step for Poisson log-likelihood)
        residuals_points = 1 .- λ_pred[1:n_points]
        residuals_quad = -λ_pred[(n_points+1):end]
        
        y_combined[1:n_points] = log_λ_pred[1:n_points] + residuals_points
        y_combined[(n_points+1):end] = log_λ_pred[(n_points+1):end] + residuals_quad ./ quadrature_weights
        
        println("Iteration $iter: Average predicted intensity = $(mean(λ_pred[1:n_points]))")
    end
    
    # Final fit
    mach = machine(rf_model, DataFrame(X_combined, :auto), y_combined, weights)
    fit!(mach)
    
    return mach
end

# Example with nonlinear intensity surface
Random.seed!(456)

# Generate nonlinear intensity function
function nonlinear_intensity(x, y)
    return exp(1.0 + sin(x) * cos(y) + 0.5 * (x-5)^2 / 25)
end

# Generate point pattern
window = (0.0, 10.0, 0.0, 10.0)
points = simulate_inhomogeneous_poisson(window, nonlinear_intensity, 1000)

# Create rich feature space including interactions
n_points = size(points, 1)
X_points = hcat(
    ones(n_points),                    # Intercept  
    points[:, 1],                      # x
    points[:, 2],                      # y  
    points[:, 1].^2,                   # x²
    points[:, 2].^2,                   # y²
    points[:, 1] .* points[:, 2],      # xy
    sin.(points[:, 1]),                # sin(x)
    cos.(points[:, 2])                 # cos(y)
)

# Quadrature grid
quad_x = repeat(0.2:0.4:9.8, inner=25)
quad_y = repeat(0.2:0.4:9.8, outer=25)
quad_points = hcat(quad_x, quad_y)
quad_weights = fill(0.16, length(quad_x))

X_quad = hcat(
    ones(size(quad_points, 1)),
    quad_points[:, 1],
    quad_points[:, 2],
    quad_points[:, 1].^2,
    quad_points[:, 2].^2,
    quad_points[:, 1] .* quad_points[:, 2],
    sin.(quad_points[:, 1]),
    cos.(quad_points[:, 2])
)

# Fit random forest
println("Fitting Random Forest for point process intensity...")
rf_fit = fit_rf_point_process(points, X_points[:, 2:end],  # Exclude intercept
                            quad_points, quad_weights, 
                            X_quad[:, 2:end])

# Compare predictions with true intensity
test_x = repeat(1:0.5:9, inner=17)
test_y = repeat(1:0.5:9, outer=17)
X_test = hcat(
    test_x, test_y,
    test_x.^2, test_y.^2, test_x .* test_y,
    sin.(test_x), cos.(test_y)
)

predicted_log_intensity = predict(rf_fit, DataFrame(X_test, :auto))
predicted_intensity = exp.(predicted_log_intensity)
true_intensity = [nonlinear_intensity(x, y) for (x, y) in zip(test_x, test_y)]

println("Random Forest R² = $(cor(predicted_intensity, true_intensity)^2)")

6.4.2 Neural Network Intensity Models

Neural networks provide even greater flexibility for modeling complex intensity surfaces. The key insight is to use the network to model log-intensity, ensuring positivity through the exponential activation.

using Flux, Optimization, OptimizationOptimJL

"""
Define neural network for point process intensity
"""
function create_intensity_network(input_dim, hidden_dims)
    layers = []
    
    # Input layer to first hidden layer
    push!(layers, Dense(input_dim, hidden_dims[1], relu))
    
    # Hidden layers
    for i in 1:(length(hidden_dims)-1)
        push!(layers, Dense(hidden_dims[i], hidden_dims[i+1], relu))
    end
    
    # Output layer (log-intensity)
    push!(layers, Dense(hidden_dims[end], 1))
    
    return Chain(layers...)
end

"""
Point process loss function for neural network
"""
function point_process_loss(network, params, points_data, quad_data, quad_weights)
    # Reconstruct network parameters
    Flux.loadparams!(network, params)
    
    # Predict log-intensity at points and quadrature
    log_λ_points = network(points_data)
    log_λ_quad = network(quad_data)
    
    # Convert to intensity
    λ_points = exp.(log_λ_points)
    λ_quad = exp.(log_λ_quad)
    
    # Negative log-likelihood (to minimize)
    nll = -sum(log_λ_points) + sum(quad_weights .* λ_quad)
    
    return nll
end

# Example: Fit neural network
Random.seed!(654)

# Use same nonlinear data as before but with normalized inputs
points_norm = (points .- 5.0) ./ 5.0  # Normalize to [-1, 1]
quad_points_norm = (quad_points .- 5.0) ./ 5.0

# Create network
network = create_intensity_network(2, [20, 10])
params_init, _ = Flux.destructure(network)

# Prepare data
points_data = points_norm'
quad_data = quad_points_norm'

# Define loss function
loss_fn(params) = point_process_loss(network, params, points_data, 
                                   quad_data, quad_weights)

# Optimization
prob = OptimizationProblem(loss_fn, params_init)
sol = solve(prob, BFGS())

println("Neural network optimization complete")
println("Final loss: $(sol.minimum)")

# Evaluate fitted model
Flux.loadparams!(network, sol.u)
test_points_norm = hcat(test_x, test_y)
test_points_norm = (test_points_norm .- 5.0) ./ 5.0
nn_log_intensity = network(test_points_norm')
nn_intensity = exp.(vec(nn_log_intensity))

println("Neural Network R² = $(cor(nn_intensity, true_intensity)^2)")

6.5 Deep Learning for Point Processes: Neural Hawkes

Traditional Hawkes processes model self-exciting point processes where past events increase the probability of future events. Neural Hawkes processes use neural networks to learn flexible triggering functions, providing much greater modeling capacity than parametric forms.

A Hawkes process has conditional intensity:

λ(t) = μ + ∫₀ᵗ φ(t - s) dN(s)

where μ > 0 is the baseline intensity, φ(·) is the triggering function, and N(s) counts events up to time s.

In Neural Hawkes, the triggering function φ is replaced by a neural network that learns from data how past events influence current intensity.

Definition 8.3 (Neural Hawkes Process). A Neural Hawkes process has conditional intensity: λ(t) = f(h(t)) where h(t) is a hidden state evolved by a recurrent neural network, and f(·) ensures positivity of the intensity.

# Simplified implementation concept for Neural Hawkes
using Flux

"""
Neural Hawkes model using LSTM for hidden state evolution
"""
struct NeuralHawkes
    lstm::LSTM
    intensity_net::Chain
    baseline::Float64
end

function NeuralHawkes(hidden_dim::Int, baseline::Float64 = 1.0)
    lstm = LSTM(1, hidden_dim)  # Input: inter-event times
    intensity_net = Chain(
        Dense(hidden_dim, hidden_dim ÷ 2, relu),
        Dense(hidden_dim ÷ 2, 1, softplus)  # Ensures positive output
    )
    
    return NeuralHawkes(lstm, intensity_net, baseline)
end

function (model::NeuralHawkes)(inter_event_times)
    # Process sequence of inter-event times
    hidden_states = model.lstm(reshape(inter_event_times, 1, :))
    
    # Compute intensities
    intensities = model.baseline .+ vec(model.intensity_net(hidden_states))
    
    return intensities
end

# Training would involve maximum likelihood estimation
# with numerical integration of the intensity function
println("Neural Hawkes model structure defined")

6.6 Model Diagnostics

Proper model validation is crucial for point process regression. Traditional residual plots from linear regression do not directly apply, but several diagnostic tools have been developed specifically for point processes.

6.6.1 Residual Analysis for Point Processes

For a fitted point process model with estimated intensity λ̂(s), we can define residuals based on the Pearson or deviance residuals used in generalized linear models.

Definition 8.4 (Point Process Residuals). For a point process model fitted by quadrature with estimated intensity λ̂(s), define:

  1. Raw residuals: rᵢ = yᵢ - μ̂ᵢ where yᵢ is the observed count and μ̂ᵢ = λ̂(sᵢ)wᵢ
  2. Pearson residuals: rᵢᴾ = (yᵢ - μ̂ᵢ)/√μ̂ᵢ
  3. Deviance residuals: rᵢᴰ = sign(yᵢ - μ̂ᵢ)√2[yᵢlog(yᵢ/μ̂ᵢ) - (yᵢ - μ̂ᵢ)]

6.6.2 Lurking Variable Plots

Lurking variable plots assess whether the fitted model adequately captures spatial variation by plotting cumulative residuals against spatial coordinates or other covariates not
